full_response:
Because the wording of the problem is very terse we first state explicitly the physical situation we shall treat and the symbols we will use.

• The primed frame S′ is, at τ = 0, the instantaneous rest-frame of the particle.  
• In that frame the spatial components of the 4-acceleration are constant,

  a′ ≡ |a′ i| = const ,  A′ μ = (0, a′, 0, 0)                        (1)

  (we have chosen the x–direction as the direction of the acceleration – nothing is lost by this choice).  
• Space–time indices are lowered and raised with the Minkowski metric ημν = diag(−1,1,1,1); we work in units c = 1 throughout.  
• We want the world-line xμ(τ) of the particle expressed solely in terms of the constant proper acceleration a′.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Equation of motion in an arbitrary inertial frame
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The definition of the 4-acceleration is

  Aμ ≡ dUμ/dτ                                                        (2)

with the constraints

  UμUμ = −1       and       UμAμ = 0.                             (3)

Because the magnitude of Aμ is Lorentz–invariant,

  AμAμ = + a′2 = const.                                           (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Choose the initial conditions
--------------------------------------------------------------------
At τ = 0 we place the particle at the origin of S (the lab frame) and let it be momentarily at rest there:

  xμ(0) = (0,0,0,0) ,   Uμ(0) = (1,0,0,0) .                       (5)

We also impose that, at τ = 0, the 4-acceleration points along +x :

  Aμ(0) = (0, a′, 0, 0) .                                          (6)

Because the 4-acceleration in the particle’s own rest–frame is (0,a′,0,0) at all times, its components in the lab frame must vary with τ in such a way that (4) and (3) stay satisfied.  One finds immediately the unique solution

  Aμ(τ) = ( a′ sinh(a′τ),  a′ cosh(a′τ), 0, 0 ).                  (7)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Integrate once – the 4-velocity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Integrating (2) with (7):

  dU0/dτ =  a′ sinh(a′τ)   →   U0(τ) =  cosh(a′τ) + C0,  
  dUx/dτ =  a′ cosh(a′τ)   →   Ux(τ) =  sinh(a′τ) + Cx,

and Uy = Uz = 0.  Using the initial conditions (5) gives C0 = 0 and Cx = 0, hence

  Uμ(τ) = ( cosh(a′τ),  sinh(a′τ), 0, 0 ).                        (8)

One readily verifies UμUμ = −1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Integrate again – the world-line
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Since Uμ = dxμ/dτ, integrate (8) once more:

  dt/dτ = cosh(a′τ)  →  t(τ) = (1/a′) sinh(a′τ) + t0 ,
  dx/dτ = sinh(a′τ)  →  x(τ) = (1/a′) cosh(a′τ) + x0 ,

and y = z = const.  Choosing the origin so that x0 = t0 = y = z = 0 yields

  t(τ) = (1/a′) sinh(a′τ),                                        (9a)  
  x(τ) = (1/a′) cosh(a′τ),                                        (9b)  
  y(τ) = z(τ) = 0.                                                (9c)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Properties of the trajectory
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Eliminating τ gives the familiar hyperbola

  x2 − t2 = 1/a′2 ,                                               (10)

which is the world-line of an observer undergoing uniform proper acceleration a′.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Restoring the speed of light
--------------------------------------------------------------------
In conventional units (c ≠ 1) replace a′ → α and τ → ατ/c to obtain

  ct(τ) = (c2/α) sinh(ατ/c),                                     (11a)  
  x(τ)  = (c2/α) cosh(ατ/c),                                     (11b)  
with  x2 − c2t2 = c4/α2.                                          (11c)

These equations display explicitly how the position of the object depends only on the constant proper acceleration α (≡ a′ in units where c = 1).

\boxed{ \;
\begin{aligned}
t(\tau) &= \frac{1}{a'}\sinh(a'\tau),\\[2pt]
x(\tau) &= \frac{1}{a'}\cosh(a'\tau),\\[2pt]
y(\tau) &= z(\tau) = 0,\qquad\qquad
x^{2}-t^{2}=a'^{-2}\;
}
 
Confidence: 55%
